retrieved_docs.title: Acarbose -- Indications -- Aging
retrieved_docs.content: Gupta S. et al conducted a 3-month study in mice to evaluate the anti-aging effects of acarbose. [8] 20-month-old C57BL/6 male and female mice were given a standard diet or a diet with 1000 ppm of acarbose. The acarbose-treated mice had decreased age-related lesions of the heart and kidney. Acarbose's anti-aging and anti-inflammatory effects appear mediated through the ERK1/2 signaling and the p38-MAPK pathways. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Bisacodyl -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Older patients: The potential adverse effects of long-term usage in older individuals suggest that stimulant laxatives, such as bisacodyl, should be used only after attempting fiber and osmotic laxatives. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Acarbose -- Administration -- Adult Dosing
retrieved_docs.content: Mabate B. et al conducted in vitro research on combining fucoidan and acarbose to treat diabetes mellitus. [13] Fucoidan is a natural product that, when combined with acarbose, produces about 70% inhibition of alpha-glucosidase. This combination could decrease the dose of acarbose needed to lower plasma glucose and thus reduce the side effects of acarbose.

retrieved_docs.title: Respiratory Syncytial Virus Prefusion F (RSVPreF3) Vaccine -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: The AS01 adjuvant contained in the RSVPreF3 vaccine is a liposomal formulation that combines 2 immunostimulants that are QS-21, extracted from the bark of the Quillaja saponaria tree, and MPL, a synthetic derivative of bacterial lipopolysaccharide ( Salmonella Minnesota strain). [15] The AS01 adjuvant enhances the immune response to the RSVPreF3 antigen by activating innate immune cells, inducing cytokine production, promoting antigen presentation, and stimulating humoral and cellular immunity. [15] Moreover, the AS01 adjuvant also helps overcome the natural decline in immunity that accompanies aging by amplifying the magnitude and quality of the antibody response. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Lactulose -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: First, the colonic metabolism of sugars causes a laxative effect via increased intraluminal gas formation and osmolality, which reduces transit time and intraluminal pH. This laxative effect is also beneficial for constipation. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Lactulose -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Contraindications to lactulose include the following: Galactosemia: Lactulose, due to its chemical composition, contains galactose and is contraindicated in patients requiring a galactose-free diet. [19] Diabetes: Although only a small fraction of lactulose undergoes systemic absorption, diabetes patients must use it cautiously due to the potential to cause hyperglycemia in diabetic individuals, as documented in a few studies. [20] [21] Older patients: Studies comparing the clinical efficacy and safety of other osmotic laxatives, such as sorbitol against lactulose, have concluded that in older patients, lactulose causes increased nausea as a side effect. Sorbitol is thus a safer and inexpensive drug to use in this population. [22] Pregnancy: Pregnancy is a stressor of hemodynamic physiology. Theoretically, via the drug's osmotic action, the prolonged use of lactulose may lead to electrolyte imbalances. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Hyaluronic Acid -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: The hyaluronic acid filler can classify as animal-derived or non-animal derived. Animal-derived fillers come from a rooster comb, and non-animal derived hyaluronic acid production occurs through biofermentation of Streptococcus . The hyaluronic acid filler can further be classified based on whether it is processed through particulate or non-particulate manufacturing. The particulate size determines the longevity of particulate manufactured hyaluronic acid filler, whereas cross-linking density determines the longevity of non-particulate manufactured hyaluronic acid filler.

retrieved_docs.title: Tropical Sprue -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Test: Fecal fat >6 g/24h on high-fat diet; abnormal D-xylose absorption

retrieved_docs.title: Laxatives -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: A cohort study utilized UK Biobank data to explore the relationship between regular laxative use and dementia incidence in adults aged 40 to 69 without prior dementia history. The study revealed that regular use of laxatives increases the risk of all-cause dementia. Increased risk was notably observed among those using multiple or osmotic laxatives. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Mannitol -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Breastfeeding considerations: There is insufficient data regarding mannitol's presence in human or animal milk and its effects on breastfed infants. When administering mannitol, it is crucial to balance the benefits of breastfeeding against the mother's clinical needs and potential risks to the infant from both the injection and the mother's condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Aging -- Pathophysiology -- Glycation
retrieved_docs.content: Advanced glycosylation end-products form when reactions occur between aldehyde groups of reducing sugars and amino groups of proteins. The formation of these metabolic products occurs in a fashion dependent on elevated blood glucose. [20] In aging individuals, glycemic control becomes less regulated, and glucose tolerance can undergo significant alteration. The predominance of advanced glycosylation end-products can result in abnormalities such as vascular fibrosis, thickened basement membranes, impaired lipid metabolism, and reduced collagenous elasticity. Furthermore, advanced glycosylation end-products are associated with the induction of inflammatory responses, resulting in the release of inflammatory substances and reactive oxygen species, causing further tissue damage. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Viscoelastics -- Preparation -- Sodium Hyaluronate
retrieved_docs.content: It is a salt derivative of hyaluronic acid and is present in the connective tissue of bacteria and humans. In homo sapiens, it is found in skin, synovial fluid, and vitreous humor. Sodium hyaluronate was first extracted from the vitreous humor of cows, and Mayer and Palmer named it because it is derived from hyaloids and contains uronic acid. The commercially available formulation is extracted from rooster coombs or streptococcal bacterial culture. Sodium hyaluronate is a long-chain mucopolysaccharide chain with a molecular weight of 2.5 to 4 million Daltons. The viscosity ranges from 100000 to 30000 centipoise with a half-life of 1 day in aqueous and three days in vitreous. The characteristic properties of sodium hyaluronate are sterile, noninflammatory, non-toxic, nonantigenic, and nonpyrogenic. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Glucosamine Sulfate -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: Glucosamine has no serious adverse effects, but possible complications include [14] : Epigastric pain Heartburn Diarrhea Nausea

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Fructose Metabolism -- Clinical Significance -- Essential Fructosuria
retrieved_docs.content: Increased fructose levels are associated with myocardial infarction and aging. Fructose glycates at a rate tenfold that of glucose, and reduced antioxidant activity contributes to myocardial infarction. [24] [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Lactulose -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy considerations: Lactulose is a pregnancy category B drug. Based on reproduction studies conducted in mice, rats, and rabbits at 2 or 4 times the usual human oral dose, there has been no evidence of impaired fertility or fetal harm due to lactulose. However, there are no adequate and well-controlled studies conducted in pregnant women. Therefore, this drug should be used during pregnancy only if needed clearly. [14] [15]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1396
retrieved_docs.content: O‑raffinose cross‑linked human hemoglobin and dextran are both classified as members of the group “blood substitutes and plasma protein fractions,” indicating that they serve as artificial blood products or plasma protein derivatives used to replace or augment the functions of red blood cells or plasma proteins in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 122
retrieved_docs.content: Blood substitutes and perfusion solutions comprise a broad therapeutic class that includes both irrigating solutions and intravenous (IV) solutions used for parenteral administration of fluids, electrolytes, nutrients, and plasma‑derived or synthetic colloids. Within this hierarchy, the subcategory “irrigating solutions, blood substitutes and perfusion” contains other irrigating solutions (e.g., glycin) and salt irrigating solutions (e.g., sodium bicarbonate). The IV‑solution branch contains formulations that affect electrolyte balance; the carbohydrate/electrolyte group links agents that combine electrolytes with carbohydrates, antidiarrheals, and intestinal anti‑inflammatory/anti‑infective drugs. The “blood substitutes and plasma protein fractions” subgroup includes O‑raffinose cross‑linked human hemoglobin, dextran, and hydroxyethyl‑starch. Dextran is further divided into dextran 40, dextran 70, and dextran 75, each representing distinct molecular‑weight fractions employed for

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 1857
retrieved_docs.content: Cisaprid is a drug that belongs to the therapeutic class of laxatives, cathartics and purgatives. Because of its pharmacologic properties, cisaprid is contraindicated for use in patients who are receiving dextran 75, a high‑molecular‑weight plasma volume expander. Consequently, clinicians should avoid co‑administration of cisaprid with dextran 75 to prevent potential adverse effects and ensure safe patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 63
retrieved_docs.content: The therapeutic category CARBOHYDRATE/ELECTROLYTES groups electrolyte‑carbohydrate solutions, antidiarrheals and intestinal anti‑inflammatory/anti‑infective agents and includes subclasses such as Solutions affecting the electrolyte balance and Solutions producing osmotic diuresis, both of which are intravenous (IV) solutions used for parenteral administration of fluids, electrolytes and nutrients. These IV solutions belong to the broader class BLOOD SUBSTITUTES AND PERFUSION SOLUTIONS, which also comprises irrigating solutions, blood substitutes, perfusion fluids and blood‑related products. Blood‑related products contain the subclass Blood substitutes and plasma protein fractions, encompassing O‑raffinose cross‑linked human hemoglobin, dextran and hydroxyethyl starch; dextran further divides into dextran 40, dextran 70 and dextran 75. Replacement preparation is a subgroup of IV solutions and dietary supplements employed in cardiac surgery and perfusion; it includes sodium

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1449
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac glycosides are a class of naturally occurring compounds that exert positive inotropic effects on the heart and are used therapeutically, for example in the treatment of certain arrhythmias and heart failure. Sucralose, on the other hand, is an artificial, non‑nutritive sweetener derived from sucrose and is employed as a sugar substitute in food and beverages. These two entities are distinct; cardiac glycosides are not sucralose, and sucralose does not possess the pharmacologic properties of cardiac glycosides.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1621
retrieved_docs.content: Glucose 100 mg/mL injection is formulated with dextrose as its pharmacologically active component; anhydrous dextrose constitutes the active moiety of the preparation, while dextrose monohydrate serves as the active ingredient. Both forms of dextrose provide the therapeutic glucose delivered by the injection, ensuring consistent dosing and efficacy in clinical use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 207
retrieved_docs.content: as a saccharide, and **beta‑D‑glucopyranose**, identified as an inverse‑isa member of the saccharide class. Collectively, these relationships delineate a network in which antidiarrheal and intestinal antiinfective agents, probiotic microorganisms, carbohydrate‑electrolyte solutions, blood substitute components, and various saccharide derivatives intersect across therapeutic, nutritional, and formulation contexts, underscoring their integrated relevance to patient care and pharmaceutical development.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1292
retrieved_docs.content: Acarbose is chemically related to trisaccharides, as it has trisaccharides as its parent structure. This classification indicates that acarbose is derived from or belongs to the trisaccharide family, which underlies its function as an alpha‑glucosidase inhibitor used to slow carbohydrate digestion in the management of diabetes. Understanding that acarbose originates from trisaccharides helps explain its mechanism of action and informs its appropriate use in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1341
retrieved_docs.content: Beta‑D‑glucopyranose is classified as a saccharide, belonging to the broad group of carbohydrate molecules known as saccharidy. As a specific sugar monomer, beta‑D‑glucopyranose exemplifies the structural and functional characteristics of saccharides, which serve as essential energy sources, metabolic intermediates, and structural components in biological systems. Recognizing beta‑D‑glucopyranose as a member of the saccharidy class aids in understanding its role in nutrition, biochemical pathways, and laboratory analyses of carbohydrate content.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 2294
retrieved_docs.content: The nicotine 4 MG Chewing Gum contains nikotin as an active ingredient and includes mannitol as an inactive ingredient, indicating that the product’s therapeutic effect is provided by nikotin while mannitol serves as a non‑active excipient in the formulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2005
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium gluceptate is recognized as a therapeutic agent that may help prevent secondary gastrointestinal bleeding, indicating its potential role in protecting the gastric and intestinal mucosa from hemorrhagic complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 569
retrieved_docs.content: Anhydrous dextrose serves as the active moiety of the medication glucose 100 MG/ML Injection, meaning it is the pharmacologically effective component of this injectable glucose solution used to provide rapid carbohydrate supplementation in clinical settings.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1350
retrieved_docs.content: Srdeční glykosidy (cardiac glycosides) and sukralfát (sucralose) are mentioned together, but established pharmacology distinguishes them as separate categories: cardiac glycosides are a class of heart‑acting drugs derived from plants, whereas sukralfát is a synthetic, non‑nutritive sweetener. The initial claim that srdeční glykosidy “is a” sukralfát appears to be a misclassification and is corrected by recognizing the two entities as unrelated in their therapeutic class. In addition, sukralfát is noted as a possible treatment for الْتِهابُ المَعِدَة (gastritis). While sucralose is primarily used as a sugar substitute, some sources suggest it may have a soothing effect on the gastric mucosa, although robust clinical evidence for its efficacy in managing gastritis is limited. Understanding the correct classification of these substances and the tentative therapeutic role of sukralfát in gastritis helps clinicians avoid confusion and consider appropriate, evidence‑based options for patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 85
retrieved_docs.content: the pharmacologically active component is anhydrous dextrose (the active moiety), while dextrose monohydrate is listed as the active ingredient; together these anhydrous and monohydrate forms constitute the essential active substance of the formulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1593
retrieved_docs.content: biosynthetic pathways related to protein and muscle growth. Finally, trisaccharides, as short carbohydrate chains, can serve as substrates or regulatory sugars that modulate glycosylation and other carbohydrate‑based biosynthetic processes. Together, these entities illustrate the wide range of chemical, pharmacologic, and biological factors that can define, regulate, or modify biosynthetic mechanisms, underscoring their importance for understanding metabolic regulation and for developing therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 774
retrieved_docs.content: Hydroxyethylový škrob and beta‑D‑glucopyranose are both classified as saccharides, meaning they belong to the broader group of carbohydrate molecules. Hydroxyethylový škrob, a chemically modified starch, is used in medical settings as a plasma volume expander and shares the fundamental carbohydrate structure of saccharides. Beta‑D‑glucopyranose represents the cyclic form of glucose, a basic monosaccharide that serves as a primary energy source in human metabolism. Recognizing that both compounds are saccharides highlights their common biochemical nature while underscoring their distinct roles in clinical practice and nutritional physiology.
